items.prompt: For a closed-interval exact search on an array of length n, what are the usual initial boundaries?

options.text: left = 0 and right = n - 1.
left = 0 and right = n.
left = 1 and right = n.
left = -1 and right = n.

feedback.reason: A closed exact-search interval contains valid candidate indices from left through right.
feedback.details: For n elements, the full candidate set is [0, n - 1].

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_n: Index n is outside a closed array interval.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_n: This skips index 0 and includes index n.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.minus_one_n: These are sentinels, not the ordinary active closed interval.

items.prompt: In ascending sorted data, nums[mid] is greater than target. Which closed-interval update preserves the exact-search invariant?

options.text: right = mid - 1.
left = mid + 1.
right = mid.
Return mid.

feedback.reason: Every index at or to the right of mid contains a value at least nums[mid], which is too large.
feedback.details: The remaining possible target region is strictly left of mid.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_mid_plus_one: This discards the only side where a smaller target may remain.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_mid: For exact search after strict greater-than, mid is known not to be the target and should be removed.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_mid: The value at mid is not equal to target.

items.prompt: In ascending sorted data, nums[mid] is less than target. Which closed-interval update is correct?

options.text: left = mid + 1.
right = mid - 1.
left = mid.
Return not found immediately.

feedback.reason: Every index at or to the left of mid contains a value no greater than nums[mid], which is too small.
feedback.details: The target, if present, must be strictly right of mid.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_mid_minus_one: This keeps only values that are no larger than nums[mid].
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_mid: Mid is known not to match and retaining it can prevent strict progress.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_not_found: The target may remain to the right.

items.prompt: What should an exact binary search do when nums[mid] equals target and the contract accepts any matching index?

options.text: Return mid immediately.
Always continue left until the first occurrence.
Always continue right until the last occurrence.
Discard mid and continue without recording it.

feedback.reason: The result contract is already satisfied by the matching midpoint.
feedback.details: Searching for first or last occurrence requires a different invariant and completion policy.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.continue_left: That implements a lower-bound-style contract rather than any-match.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.continue_right: That implements a different boundary contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_mid: This can lose the known valid answer.

items.prompt: What should a correct closed-interval exact search return after the loop exits with left > right?

options.text: The target is not present in the searched array.
left is always a matching target index.
right is always a matching target index.
The last midpoint must be returned.

feedback.reason: The invariant says every possible target index remains inside [left, right].
feedback.details: When that interval becomes empty, no candidate position remains.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_index: For exact search, left may be only an insertion position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_index: The interval may be empty with no match.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_index: The last midpoint may contain a nonmatching value.

items.prompt: How should an iterative exact search behave on an empty array?

options.text: The initial interval is empty, so it returns not found without reading an element.
It reads index 0 once before returning.
It returns index 0 as the insertion point under the exact-index contract.
It must throw because binary search cannot represent emptiness.

feedback.reason: With left = 0 and right = -1, the loop condition left <= right is false.
feedback.details: A correct interval convention handles empty input naturally.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_zero: The contract asks for a matching index, not insertion position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.throw_always: An empty interval is a normal searchable state.

items.prompt: A one-element array contains target. What should happen on the first iteration?

options.text: mid is 0, the value matches, and index 0 is returned.
The loop is skipped because left equals right.
right becomes -1 before the value is checked.
Index 1 is returned because length is 1.

feedback.reason: A singleton closed interval must be inspected.
feedback.details: This is a critical edge case for distinguishing left <= right from left < right.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_loop: A closed interval with left == right still contains one candidate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_negative: The midpoint must be evaluated first.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_one: Length and last valid index are different.

items.prompt: The sorted array contains duplicate target values, and the contract accepts any matching index. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Returning whichever matching midpoint is encountered is valid.
The algorithm must return the first occurrence.
The algorithm must return the last occurrence.
Duplicates make exact binary search incorrect.

feedback.reason: The invariant only needs to preserve the possibility of some target index.
feedback.details: Duplicates matter when the contract asks for a specific boundary, not when any match is acceptable.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_first: That is not required by the stated contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_last: That is a different result contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_break_all: Equality still identifies a valid match.

items.prompt: Which midpoint formula avoids overflow in fixed-width integer arithmetic when left and right are nonnegative?

options.text: left + (right - left) / 2.
(left + right) / 2 without any overflow consideration.
left * right / 2.
(right - left) / 2 without adding left.

feedback.reason: Computing the interval offset before adding it to left avoids summing two large boundaries.
feedback.details: The exact integer-rounding convention must still match the update rules.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sum_mid: The sum can overflow even when both boundaries are valid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.product_mid: This is not the interval midpoint and can overflow more easily.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.difference_only: This yields an offset rather than the absolute midpoint index.

items.prompt: An exact search compares target with nums[mid] using an ordering inconsistent with how the array was sorted. What is the main defect?

options.text: The direction chosen after comparison no longer preserves the target-candidate invariant.
The search remains correct but becomes slower.
The defect appears only when duplicates exist.
The midpoint necessarily overflows.

feedback.reason: The search order and comparison relation must agree.
feedback.details: Binary search correctness depends on knowing how values on each side relate to nums[mid].

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_slower: An inconsistent comparator can discard the side containing the target.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_only: Comparator inconsistency can fail on distinct values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_overflow: Comparator semantics are unrelated to midpoint arithmetic.

items.prompt: Which statements belong to the classic exact-search invariant? Select all that apply.

options.text: If target exists, at least one matching index remains inside the active interval.
Every discarded index is proven unable to contain target.
Every nonterminal update removes mid and strictly shrinks the interval.
The interval always contains the first occurrence specifically.

feedback.reason: Classic exact search preserves possible membership and removes known-impossible regions.
feedback.details: It does not promise first or last occurrence unless the contract is strengthened.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_occurrence: That stronger boundary invariant is not required for any-match search.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.target_inside: This is the core candidate invariant.

items.prompt: Which test cases are important for classic exact binary search? Select all that apply.

options.text: Empty input.
A singleton containing and not containing target.
Target at the first and last index.
Only one odd-length case where target starts at the midpoint.

feedback.reason: Boundary, absence, and minimal-size cases expose most invariant defects.
feedback.details: A midpoint hit alone can pass even when every update rule is wrong.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_middle: This does not exercise updates or edge boundaries.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.empty: It verifies the initial empty interval.

items.prompt: Which updates are valid for closed-interval exact search after a strict comparison? Select all that apply.

options.text: If nums[mid] < target, set left = mid + 1.
If nums[mid] > target, set right = mid - 1.
If nums[mid] == target, return mid under an any-match contract.
If nums[mid] < target, set left = mid.

feedback.reason: Each branch either returns a valid answer or removes the known-nonmatching midpoint with the impossible side.
feedback.details: Keeping mid after a strict comparison breaks strict progress.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.too_small_keep_mid: Mid is known wrong and retaining it can stall on a singleton interval.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.too_small: Mid and everything left of it are too small.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.too_large: Mid and everything right of it are too large.

items.prompt: Arrange one iteration of classic exact binary search.

elements.text: Confirm the active closed interval is non-empty.
Compute a midpoint inside the interval.
Return on equality; otherwise discard the proven-impossible side including mid.

feedback.reason: The update decision follows midpoint evidence inside a valid active interval.
feedback.details: Every nonterminal branch must preserve the invariant and shrink the interval.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning after nums[mid] is less than target in ascending data.

elements.text: Establish that mid is not a match and is too small.
Use sorted order to show every index at or left of mid is also too small.

feedback.reason: The boundary update is justified by a chain from local comparison to regional elimination.
feedback.details: This reasoning is the half-discard proof for exact ascending search.

items.prompt: Arrange the debugging steps for an exact search that misses a target at the final index.

elements.text: State whether the active interval is closed or half-open.
Trace the iteration where only the final index remains.
Verify that the loop condition still processes a one-element interval.
Verify earlier updates did not discard the final index incorrectly.

feedback.reason: Off-by-one debugging starts from the interval contract and the smallest failing state.
feedback.details: The final-index case often exposes a left < right loop used with a closed interval or an over-aggressive right update.

items.prompt: An iterative exact binary search runs on n sorted elements with O(1) comparisons per iteration. Select its worst-case time and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: The active interval is reduced by about half after each mismatch.

feedback.reason: Strict interval halving gives logarithmic iterations, and iterative boundaries require constant extra state.
feedback.details: This excludes preprocessing such as sorting; the input is already sorted.

items.prompt: A recursive exact binary search on n sorted elements performs O(1) work per call. Select its worst-case time and recursion-stack space.
constraints: Each recursive call searches at most half of the previous interval.

feedback.reason: Recursive and iterative versions visit logarithmically many intervals, but recursion retains one frame per level.
feedback.details: The asymptotic search time is unchanged while auxiliary stack usage differs.
